item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: welcome to wikidotTMitjustworks.com

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus. I mean, it's not as if there weren't established standards for those sort of things already, so that the devs had to make their own completely idiosyncratic version from scratch… oh wait

(I hate Markdown as a wholly unnecessary anti-innovation at the best of times, like, when it actually works in a straightforward manner, because it messes up all kinds of typical online text emphasis and text char formatting conventions. When it's malfunctioning, I want to feed someone their own face)

And it wasn't even getting multiple line breaks that was the issue. I couldn't get any. The two sentences running-on into each other that I had originally, without even a character space between them, were meant to be on wholly separate lines with whitespace between them. For some reason, it chose to completely ignore the CR/LFs for just that one part of the text.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: yeah, they only way to have multiple line breaks is to use "@@ @@" which is wikidot's escape parser. id assume the comments have the same options as the page because the devs are lazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, works nicely. Got a few other comments I need to track down and incorporate that into.

It'd be nice if that was an option for the edit box or spelled out clearly somewhere given that it's a pretty fundamental feature (maybe it's on the wikitext reference page but that's long and convoluted), and "two pairs of at-signs with a space in between" isn't exactly a standard or particularly intuitive way of eliciting the effect.

Not that it should be something I have to do given that I'd put regular linebreaks in with the enter key, same as what normally works within comments, but the software was just ignoring them no matter how many I added. Bit of a stupid bug there. Behaviour that changes pretty much at random, depending on the presence or absence of other (seemingly closed-off) formatting features further up the page.

Also, why is the comments page edit box offering me clickable-button features that don't work on these pages, without even an explanation/warning of that? The comment template is separate from that of the main pages AFAIK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
html blocks only work in pages. use

 @@ @@

for line breaks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Engrossing, the link to 4700 is pretty smart, and I really like the way it's an unspoken explanation for the prevalence of celtic knots in bronze age decorations around the North and Irish sea regions.

As ever a couple of small proofing observations:
1/ bit of an odd sentence towards the end of the second log, "various scale pigmentation's"
+ show block
– hide block

- ignoring the misplaced apostrophe, this would probably scan better as "variously pigmented scales". Also the gravity is maybe better described as being "at zero" rather than "at 0", most style guides discourage the use of inline ordinal numbers for small quantities, and with the page formatting as it is - at least on my 1280x800 monitor, in Chrome/Win7 - it ends up as an orphan on its own line. Though the first correction may fix that and the expansion may then reverse the fix,

so, YMMV.

2/ You have a ".txt" file with images and variable font, justification and divider formatting.
+ show block
– hide block

TXT is well established as containing plain ASCII text only, and it seems odd that the Foundation would create their own version which varies from this. There are plenty of alternatives that would suffice, however - you could go with the MS standard .DOC or .DOCX (depending how much the Foundation has modernised all its computers), the surprisingly also Microsoft but more "open" .RTF (it's somewhat deprecated these days, though), the OpenOffice .ODT (open document format, text), or even just good old .PDF. Or of course some random bunch of letters signifying an entirely novel Foundation-specific document type, seeing as there's already ".log"s (which are themselves usually plaintext, but it's not exactly a standard and variants exist which e.g. include ODT-like XML tags).

edit: what the actual ████ is going on with the formatting of these comments, honestly. —Can a guy get a line break?! [DATA REDACTED]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this one but it left me wondering what happened to EoI-3073-1 after retrival,its said that she is brought to some scp in attemp to recover and questioning but thats it, did I miss something?did she die after?I was expecting some interview document or something

item.title: Re: D-Class
item.content:encoded:
Like weryllium said, D-class terminations are fairly wasteful, and dont fit into my headcanon of the Foundation using and recycling its resources.

As for the semi-colons, I will try to fix them but unless I know where Im using it incorrectly its going to be hard to find them all given the size of the article.

item.title: Re: D-Class
item.content:encoded: These days most people don't really headcanon that as happening anymore, due to the sheer wastefulness that would entail.

item.title: D-Class
item.content:encoded:
Aren't D-Class Personnel terminated at the end of the month?

E: Also, you used semicolons incorrectly several times. I would fix them myself, but I need to get back to class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thaumiel doesn't represent threat level. Thaumiels are actively helpful to normalcy. You're thinking of Apollyon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The banner image was created and released by Kath under creative commons liscence.

The Celtic Knots image is something I'm attempting to backtrace, but appears to be, otherwise, within the public domain. The image appears to originate from here https://www.ireland-fun-facts.com/celticknotmeanings.html .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm in the process of reworking the description, hopefully I'll have changes up very soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with Petrograd above me, and have painstakingly developed a comprehensive list of why this gets my -1. Please take this as constructive criticism. Also, I may have a collapsable addiction…

Object Class: Thaumiel
should be nerfed to euclid

The Finnlady is your keter, and whatever was hidden in the nebula is potentially a Thaumial level threat, but the gates? Not even close to keter. While not completely understood and probably needs some TLC, there is not a scrap of evidence of the immediate keter-level threat until the reactivation log, which changes things so dramatically that we would already be in a thaumial level situation, which makes the following containment (secrecy) procedures and coy anomaly-probing close to irrelevent.

Containment

Experimentation
Not sure how I feel about that word

Testing clearance should be lowered. From what we've seen, the Foundation either understands it and knows how to repair it or it doesn't and is going to keep throwing subjects and sensors at it. Further attempts to communicate with the entity should be a top priority, as is the nature the secondary and teritary anomalous effects. This is certainly worth a few D-class.

Since going inside, though, these things seem like they might be a waste of time or under even heavier lockdown.

CCTV Cameras
can't be used

Not to moniter a skip giving off visual cognitohazards, anyway. I'd lose the visual component of the hazard anyway, since a pile of corpses spelling things with their fingers is just silly. In fact, it just made me angry when it was expunged because whatever it was would have to be described vaguely anyway, which would already remove the hazard. If whatever it was were still potent after that degree of seperation, it would be a "memetic." That said, I could totally see how the whisper-choreographed bone dance would be deeply unsettling.

VERITAS
Vital Energy Radiation Imaging Tactical Awareness System

Had no idea what these were. I'm surprised I haven't looked into the GOC that much. That said, professional documents spell out acronyms the first time they are used in the instance. Also, they wouldn't be "VERITAS readings," they'd using VERITAS to read Aetheric Energy Fields (AEF), or Elan Vital Energy (EVE), so it's "changes in baseline AEF or EVE" that they'd be looking for.

Description

Naming Order
... is just my personal preference

SCPs should be named by order of discovered layers: SCP-3703 should be the chamber, as it contains all the things you have to worry about. This includes the corpse jello, the whispers, the cold fire, and the Gigeresque tubes. SCP-3703-1 would be the most important item, but I should think one would designate the mana burn-off as a part of the skip which seems to be escaping containment at an alarming rate and no-one seems to have tried to analyse what it is or where it goes.

These Numbers
...don't add up

The initial containment protocol establishes there are 6 sites with a total of 13 instances of floating fish people. Shortly thereafter it becomes hundreds of structures. It just doesn't tell the right story.

I'd stick with six, but I'll explain that in my last suggestion.

The Symbol
...is just too much

On top of whether or not they are floating or the dimensions of the mass, the bodies are arranged in the shape of a protective seal, sigil, or glyph. Personally, I find this makes the whole thing just way too complicated. I mean now you gotta account for scale and the size of the bodies and the thickness of the lines and how many bodies can you glue together to get a reasonably legible consistency and whether or not the shapes are contiguous and I don't need to be worrying about all that.

Auditory Stimuli
...got forgotten?

It doesn't seem like audio-stimus reaction ever comes into play, either in the experiment logs or otherwise. Also, "emulating" might not be the right word. Overall, I'd cut it.

Physical Stimuli
...isn't too bad

It's just worded painfully: no non-physical objects were presented, nor is there much difference between them and the animated drone later, so just shorten it to "Items placed within 3m of SCP-3703 result in the extension of corpses to form appendages to gasp, examine, and manipulate these foreign objects before briefly internalizing them."

Plasma
...is curious

Though I can't be certain this is it's purpose, per se, the strange mists, vapors, and low-energy plasmas (like swamp gas and willow-the-whisps) venting off this thing are intriguing if not worrysome. There is no way we could have gotten that close to the thing without first sampling the air to see if it was breathable or figure out what that cold fire was.
And sorry to get technical, but there is no "energy generation" going on here. It is colder than ambient, which means thermal energy has been removed. That energy may have gone into turning the vapor into a plasma, but then it would give that energy off when you touched it, and this is fiction so screw the thermodynamics, I just have an issue whether fishlady is venting or if these things are supposed to be her batteries in which case it's going the wrong way…

Experiments and Exploration

These were all pretty good…

Experiments
...I liked

I feel like an obscure runic text from an unknown culture are too easily translated, but whatevs
The square peg without a square hole was too much fun! Almost got my upvote there.
The part with the mice was good; I appreciate you not wasting D-class (who were well written, btw).
Nice work with the retrieval cables, too. So many researchers forget those. Basically, I think this section is really good.

Exploration
...was a little purple

The roboticist working with Dr. Alva is obviously having way too much fun. I'm all for an irrepressible engineer, but there was no way of knowing there wouldn't be gravity in there, so going ahead and giving the drone jet thrusters made no sense. I mean what are they? Compressed air? Ion repulsors? Either way that is a substancial weight that no sane explorer would throw on a drone that has to explore an unknown distance on limited energy supplies. Maybe saying this was developed ahead of time in preparation for dodging the skip's grabby hands might justify it, particularly if that limited supply had enough left over to set it drifting down that tunnel. There should also probably be an adjustment period as the pilot discovers there is no gravity, which would completely disrupt the drone's ability to fly.

I'm not sure we need to know the colors of all the things. That said, when did the plasma turn into purple fluid? This might just be a problem with my headcanon, but plasma glows like lightning, like fire, like balls of blue-white energy rolling around the roof of your microwave before popping violently. "Purple fluid" is not that. Purple fluid sounds like a bunch of rancid wine is pooring down this well to drip down on gillgirl.

We know what space looks like. Don't describe it to us.

Document A and Reactivation Log
**NOPE**
FINAL WORD
I hope you actually listen to me...

I can currently only vote you down once and ultimately the moste you can get out of me is a two vote swing, so you don't have to listen to me tear your baby a new one, but I really am trying to help.

I honestly think you should play off som parallels with the Sarkic Cult vs CotBG mythos, particularly SCP-2688. The corporeal thaumaturgy, primordial sea goddesses, the six Archons…
This somehow fits into established mythology, whether by another perspective or through another culture, I see a parallel waiting to be fulfilled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of things I deeply enjoy about this (e.g., the mental image of 3703/the finfolk, the implications about Alva/history/the Foundation, the carved dialogue, the mythological beasts), and some things that didn't engage me much (the war of the Gods), but there are two major problems that… honestly are leading me all the way to a downvote.

+ show block
– hide block

First, the description is a mess. The base anomaly is thoroughly complicated, and not broken down in a clear and understandable manner. I had to read the entire article and then reread the description twice before I got the full picture.
- Describing 3703s as structures before talking about the structure they are found in (dash1) gave me the complete wrong initial mental image.
- Describing their geometric/thaumaturgical shape and the presence of extradimensional portals, THEN their ability to interact with things, THEN the plasma generation caused confusion about what was important to know/remember, as did not designating the plasma with a letter or number.
- (Also having both -A and -1 was… not a major problem, but it didn't help me zoning out a couple paragraphs in, especially as the list of effects stacked higher and higher.)
- It feels like a lot of the effects (the manipulating appendages and cognitohazards especially) were basically typed in in the order that they showed up in the later story, and thus it feels like they had too much detail or were largely superfluous. Yes, the Foundation would document them like you have, but you aren't the Foundation, and you've got a bigger story to tell.

Second big problem was Document A. This was a gigantic failure of show-don't-tell, and left me rolling my eyes for the entire duration. It was basically an attempt to explain the entire premise to the reader, and isn't even remotely an interesting interpretation. I would've been far happier with my own theories.

Look… this is already a decadent exercise in rich, mythological storytelling, using a complex narrative with many subtle references. That is absolutely not a problem, but you can't afford to do both that and be indulgent or loose in other areas. -1

Improving the description would get me a fair distance towards upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I can't say that you've perfected the draft's flow, I'm more than happy to admit that the skip has come a long way. There's this heart to the work, a very sincere emotional core that you've built, and you clearly took all measures to highlight it. Take the +1, it's well deserved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It sure has! I accept this knacksterpiece rating with honor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This sure has come a long way from July's soul crystals eh? 10/5 Knacksterpiece :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has improved a lot over the course of your working on it and I think it's great that you put the time in to make this meet its potential. The last note is kind of a meh bit to end on, but the preceding parts have a lot of nice imagery and language to keep the reader's interest. "But they need me." from the interview was a particularly good line. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks to the immense amount of critt and support I got for this thing from:
Roget
Weryllium - Weryllium is a constant source of good critt, and insight into what does and doesn't work in my drafts.
Modern_Erasmus - For direction and conceptual critt.
DrMagnus - For critt and format stealing.
A Random Day
psul - For giving me the massive narrative direction push I needed
mlister - For narrative, tone, and internal structure critt. Not everything they gave me I could adequately use, or effectively execute into the draft, but it's given me the insight I need for moving forward in this canon.
9Volt - A constant wellspring of ideas for any draft I put on the table.
The Great Hippo - For tearing the draft apart, and putting it together again right in front of my eyes, in a way that was both insightful to how to execute in the future, and where my shortcomings are.
Tufto
Mortos
Woedenaz
not_a_seagull
JuDaS
taylor_itkin
djkaktus - For formatting help.
BananaRepublic - For critt
tretter

This is a continuation of the Orcadian Saga, and something I spent the last two full months working on. It started off as crystals made from Finnfolk sacrificing themselves, and evolved into clusters of corpses, where the original themes were unclear it evolved to focus on sacrifice, loss, and desperation. This is one step closer to my 001 proposal. Thanks for all the help.

Other Articles within this Canon
Hide Stuff
  • SCP-3456 The Orcadian Horseme
  • SCP-3047 To Be Drowned By A Train
  • SCP-3700 Tides of War
  • SCP-3702 I Walk Through The Desert, And I Have No Name
  • The Seas of Orcadia Part 1: How I Met Your Mither
  • SCP-2368 Across The Water Related but as of yet not directly connected.
Warning spoilers and explanation
hide spoilers

The corpse blobs are being partially controlled by the finnwoman, whose name is Triemides. She is able to remotely control some aspects of the corpses, mostly relating to the subliminal messaging and communication attempts via the carvings on various objects. She's using this to send cries for help in whatever way she can, due to the fact that she's losing a 5000 year fight against Teran, partially because of human inflicted damage on the sites that the corpse blobs are located at. This is seen at the end of the SCP. As for the purpose of the corpse balls themselves, they are holding open a trans-planar tunnel which is being used to power my 001.

It's implied at the end that Alva is not actually who she seems to be. In Orcadian myth, there is a deity know as The Mither o' Sea, and another Deity known as Teran. They fight an endless cycle of dominance with one another (A theme which is heavily prevalent in 3700) and this is another piece where that theme is coming into play. Alva, is the Mither in human form, and the Fishwoman's message at the end reveals this, and the large humanoid entity is Teran.

There are two themes running in this piece, which consist of Triemides desperation, and the sacrifices that the Finnfolk made to keep this and 5 other deities sealed (I.E. literally sacrificing themselves in large numbers to accomplish this goal.)
